text: I sat in the waiting room before the interview, crossing and uncrossing my legs. I had rehearsed my answers fifty times but they all evaporated from my memory. My palms were sweating and I wiped them on my pants every thirty seconds. The receptionist smiled at me and I tried to smile back but it felt crooked. My mouth was dry and I could not find my water bottle.

I checked my presentation slides for the twelfth time. The meeting was in twenty minutes. I kept clicking through them, finding tiny things to adjust -- a font here, an alignment there. My stomach was doing slow flips. I went to the bathroom and practiced my opening line in the mirror. My voice sounded thin and strange. I straightened my collar and noticed my hands were trembling.

I stood backstage and heard the audience filling the seats. Hundreds of conversations blurred into a roar. I peeked through the curtain and the seats were nearly full. My script felt like wet paper in my hands. I could feel my heartbeat in my fingers. The stage manager said five minutes and the words hit me like a cold wave. I started pacing in a tight circle.

I parallel parked during my driving test with the examiner staring at my hands. My foot kept twitching on the pedal. I turned the wheel too far, then over-corrected. The cone was right there in my mirror. I could feel sweat rolling down my spine. The examiner made a note on his clipboard and I heard the pen scratch and my stomach clenched.

I refreshed my email every ninety seconds waiting for the test results. The lab said they would be posted by noon. It was eleven forty-seven. I could not sit still. I reorganized my desk, then put everything back where it was. I made coffee I did not drink. I picked up a book and read the same paragraph four times without absorbing a single word.

I rang the doorbell at his parents' house and heard footsteps approaching. This was the first time meeting them. I was holding flowers and wine and my grip on both was too tight. I checked my reflection in the glass door. Was my shirt wrinkled? Was there something in my teeth? The lock turned and my stomach launched itself into my throat.

I sat in the dentist's chair and heard the drill starting up in the next room. The hygienist clipped the paper bib around my neck. The overhead light was blindingly bright. I gripped the armrests and my knuckles went white. She asked me to open wide and I did but my jaw was trembling. I pressed my tongue against the roof of my mouth and tried to breathe through my nose.

I walked up to the podium at the open mic night and adjusted the microphone. The spotlight was hot on my face. I looked at my notebook and the words blurred. I could hear my own breathing through the speakers. Someone coughed in the audience. I cleared my throat twice. My leg was bouncing under the podium and I pressed my foot flat on the floor to stop it.

I sent the text and immediately wished I could take it back. It was too forward. Or maybe not forward enough. I put the phone face down on the table. Then picked it up. No reply yet. I set it down again. My stomach was a washing machine on spin cycle. I went for a walk and brought the phone with me, checking it every half block.

I stood at the edge of the high dive and looked down. The pool was a small blue rectangle far below. The board bounced slightly under my weight. Behind me, kids were waiting. I could not go back. My toes curled over the edge and my calves were tight. I took one breath, then another, then another, each one faster than the last.

I arrived at the party where I did not know anyone except the host. She waved from across the room and went back to her conversation. I stood near the door holding my drink with both hands. Everyone seemed to already be in groups. I studied the bookshelf like it was the most interesting thing in the world. My face felt hot and I could not figure out what to do with my arms.

I opened the exam booklet and my mind went blank. I knew this material. I had studied for weeks. But the first question sat on the page like a foreign language. The clock on the wall ticked and I could hear other students already writing. I squeezed the pen and forced myself to read the question again, word by word. The ink on the page seemed to vibrate.

I waited for my name to be called at the audition. Forty people sat around me, all warming up their voices. Some of them looked like professionals. I ran through the song in my head and forgot the second verse. My throat felt tight, like someone had their hand on it. I sipped water but it did not help. My turn was three names away.

I held the ring in my pocket and felt its edges with my thumb. She was sitting across the table, laughing at something the waiter said. The restaurant was loud but all I could hear was my own pulse. The plan was to do it during dessert. Dessert was coming. I rehearsed the words in my head and each time they came out differently. My knee was bouncing under the table.

I stood in front of the classroom on my first day as a teacher. Thirty teenagers stared at me. Some looked bored, some looked hostile, one was asleep. I wrote my name on the board and the chalk squeaked. I turned back to face them and my prepared introduction vanished. My voice came out higher than normal. I could feel every pair of eyes on me like pinpoints of heat.

I buckled my seatbelt for my first solo flight. The instructor was on the ground, watching from the taxiway. The runway stretched out ahead of me, empty. I ran through the checklist three times. My radio call stuttered. I pushed the throttle forward and felt the plane accelerate. My hands were slick on the yoke and my stomach was somewhere near my ankles.

I hit publish on the blog post and closed my laptop. Then opened it. Then closed it again. I had written about something personal and now it was out there for anyone to read. My skin prickled. I checked the analytics page: three views already. Three strangers reading my thoughts. I got up and walked around the apartment, unable to settle anywhere.

I walked into the courtroom for the first time. The ceilings were high and every sound echoed. My lawyer motioned me to sit. I pulled out the chair and it scraped loudly on the floor. The judge was reading something. I smoothed my tie. My throat was dry. I kept swallowing but there was nothing to swallow. The bailiff called a number and my lawyer stood up.

I loaded my boxes into the car and drove toward a city where I knew no one. The GPS said fourteen hours. Every mile felt like an erasure of everything familiar. I stopped at a gas station and stood by the pump, watching cars pass on the highway. My chest felt tight, not from sadness, but from the enormous blankness of what was ahead. I got back in and kept driving.

I dialed the number for the radio call-in contest and it started ringing. I had never been on the radio before. The DJ picked up and his voice was suddenly loud and real in my ear. He asked my name and I heard it come back through the speakers a half-second later, slightly distorted. My mouth was moving but I was not sure what I was saying.

I stood at the free throw line with the game tied. The gym was roaring. I bounced the ball three times, the way I always do. But my fingers felt thick and clumsy. The rim looked smaller than usual. I could feel the sweat on my forehead. The referee handed me the ball and the noise faded into a high-pitched hum. I bent my knees and my legs felt like jelly.

I set the smoke alarm off trying to cook for him. It was our first date at my apartment and the kitchen was filling with smoke. I opened every window and fanned the air with a towel while the alarm screamed. He was going to be here in twenty minutes. I scraped the burned layer off the chicken and started over, my hands jittery and my face flushed from more than the heat.

I pressed submit on the college application and the screen said thank you. That was it. Four years of grades, three drafts of the essay, two recommendation letters, all compressed into a single click. I sat back and my stomach was a knot. I closed my laptop and opened it again. The confirmation page was still there. Now I had to wait. Waiting was going to be the hard part.

I walked into the MRI clinic and the technician explained the procedure. I would lie still for forty-five minutes inside a narrow tube. The machine would be loud. I changed into the gown and lay down on the sliding table. The technician gave me foam earplugs and a panic button. The table slid in and the ceiling was inches from my nose. My breath came fast and shallow.

I watched the pregnancy test develop. Two minutes, the box said. I set it on the counter and stared at the ceiling. Then at the floor. Then at the test. Only thirty seconds had passed. My hands were shaking. I paced the tiny bathroom in three steps each direction. The window was a small bright square above me. I picked up the test and the line was starting to appear.
